displayName: Stowers Policy Limits Demand (Personal Injury — Texas)
template:
**DOCUMENT FORMAT — STOWERS POLICY LIMITS DEMAND (PI — TEXAS)**:
A time-limited, unconditional offer to settle the plaintiff's personal-injury claim within the available liability-policy limits, expressly invoking the Texas third-party bad-faith framework established in **G.A. Stowers Furniture Co. v. American Indemnity Co., 15 S.W.2d 544 (Tex. Comm'n App. 1929, holding approved)** and refined in **American Physicians Insurance Exchange v. Garcia, 876 S.W.2d 842 (Tex. 1994)** and **Phillips v. Bramlett, 288 S.W.3d 876 (Tex. 2009)**. The four-element Stowers framework is the structural backbone of this letter. Target length: 4–7 pages.

**WHAT THIS TEMPLATE IS — AND WHAT IT IS NOT**:
This is the TEXAS-SPECIFIC policy-limits demand, designed to satisfy each of the four Stowers elements with on-the-record evidence and to layer the Tex. Ins. Code § 541 (Unfair Settlement Practices) and § 542 (Prompt Payment of Claims Act) extra-contractual frameworks on top. It is NOT the generic policy-limits demand (`policy_limits_demand_pi`) — Stowers has stricter element requirements than Crisci-family doctrine. It is NOT a § 542A health-care-liability claim notice (separate Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 74.051 framework). Aliases for this template MUST NOT include generic forms (`policy_limits_demand`) without TX-specific suffix to avoid resolver collision with the trunk template.

**DYNAMIC BEHAVIOR — READ documentOptions**:
- `subPractice`: `mva` (default; majority Texas Stowers context) | `premises` | `product_liability`
- `policyTypes`: array — `auto_liability`, `umbrella`, `commercial_liability`, `homeowners`
- `responseDeadlineDays`: numeric (default 30; Stowers does not impose a statutory window, but 30 days is the empirically-tested safe range)
- `damagesCategories`: array — same set as `demand_letter_pi`
- `liabilityClarity`: `clear_and_uncontested` (default) | `clear_with_minor_dispute` (weaker but still viable) | `disputed` (Stowers may not be available — reconsider)
- `requestPolicyLimitsDisclosure`: boolean (default true) — Tex. Ins. Code § 1952.110 (auto disclosure on demand)
- `includeChapter541Predicate`: boolean (default true) — overlay Tex. Ins. Code § 541 unfair-settlement-practices framework
- `includeChapter542Predicate`: boolean (default true) — overlay Tex. Ins. Code § 542 (Prompt Payment of Claims Act) framework
- `acceptanceMechanics`: `cashier_check_to_firm_within_window` (default) | `release_and_check_simultaneous_exchange`
- `reservationOfRightsResponse`: boolean (default false) — when carrier has issued a ROR, include a paragraph addressing it

**STRUCTURE** (follow line by line):

1. Date line.
2. VIA line (bold) — `**VIA Certified Mail — Return Receipt Requested AND Email**` (default; certified-mail receipt is critical to establishing the 'demand' and 'opportunity to accept' elements of Stowers).
3. Recipient block — defendant carrier; claims department; address; adjuster name + ID; supervisor copy.
4. Re: block (multi-line, aligned labels):
   Re:    Our Client:           [client name]
          Your Insured:         [defendant name]
          Claim Number:         [claim number]
          Date of Loss:         [date of incident]
          Subject:              **STOWERS POLICY LIMITS SETTLEMENT DEMAND — TIME LIMITED**
5. Greeting — `Dear [Adjuster name]:` if known, else `Dear Claims Manager:`.

**BODY SECTIONS** (in order):

### I. Introduction and Stowers Framing
One paragraph: this letter is a formal Stowers settlement demand. It is time-limited, unconditional, and structured to satisfy each of the four elements of **G.A. Stowers Furniture Co. v. American Indemnity Co., 15 S.W.2d 544 (Tex. Comm'n App. 1929, holding approved)**, as refined in **American Physicians Insurance Exchange v. Garcia, 876 S.W.2d 842 (Tex. 1994)** and reaffirmed in **Phillips v. Bramlett, 288 S.W.3d 876 (Tex. 2009)**. Upon timely acceptance and tender of the available limits within the period stated below, plaintiff will execute a release of the named insured for all claims arising from this incident, subject to standard release language (no admission of liability; no broader release of unrelated claims; no release of UM/UIM claims against plaintiff's own carrier; no release of non-insured tortfeasors).

### II. The Four-Element Stowers Framework
A structured demonstration that each element is satisfied:

**Element 1 — The claim is within the scope of coverage.**
One paragraph: confirm the loss is covered by the policy at issue (named insured, scope of operations, date of loss within policy period, no apparent exclusion). Where any reservation-of-rights letter has been issued, address the basis (and if `reservationOfRightsResponse=true`, include a sub-paragraph rebutting the asserted exclusion).

**Element 2 — Liability is clear (a reasonably-prudent insurer would consider liability undisputed).**
Two to three paragraphs setting out the liability foundation. Adapt by `subPractice`:
- `mva`: cite Texas Transportation Code provisions for the at-fault driver's traffic-law violation (e.g., Tex. Transp. Code § 545.062 for following too closely, § 545.401 for reckless driving, § 545.351 for unsafe speed); identify point of impact, mechanism, and Texas Peace Officer's Crash Report (CR-3) number. Quote any admission against interest.
- `premises`: identify the dangerous condition; the duty owed under Texas premises-liability framework (invitee under **Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Reece, 81 S.W.3d 812 (Tex. 2002)**; constructive-notice analysis); duration of the condition; quoted incident report.
- `product_liability`: identify product, defect, recall history; cite the strict-liability framework under Texas law (recognizing **Restatement (Second) § 402A** as adopted in Texas).
When `liabilityClarity=clear_with_minor_dispute`, identify and dispose of any contested-liability theory the carrier has previously raised. When `liabilityClarity=disputed`, **Stowers may not be available — reconsider whether `demand_letter_pi` is the appropriate instrument**.

**Element 3 — Damages substantially exceed the available policy limits.**
Damages section, more rigorous than `demand_letter_pi`. Emit one sub-paragraph per `damagesCategories` entry:
- **Medical Expenses (incurred)**: itemize provider, dates of service, total billed (account for **Haygood v. Escabedo, 356 S.W.3d 390 (Tex. 2011)** paid-or-incurred limitation — recoverable past medical damages capped at amounts actually paid plus amounts insured/payor obligated to pay).
- **Future Medical Expenses**: cite the treating-physician narrative report and any retained life-care planner / economist report.
- **Lost Wages (incurred)** and **Future Earning Capacity**: documented foundation; vocational/economist projection.
- **Physical Pain, Mental Anguish, Disfigurement, Physical Impairment, Loss of Enjoyment of Life**: 1–2 narrative paragraphs personalizing impact (Texas non-economic damages framework distinguishes physical pain, mental anguish, disfigurement, physical impairment as separate categories).
- **Loss of Consortium**: per-claimant breakdown.
- **Property Damage**: estimate or total-loss valuation.
Close with `**TOTAL DAMAGES: $______**` (bold) AND a comparative line: '**Total damages substantially exceed the available policy limits, satisfying the third Stowers element.**' State the damages-to-limits ratio explicitly.

**Element 4 — Ordinary care would have led a reasonably-prudent insurer to accept.**
One paragraph: 'Given the clear liability and the damages-to-limits ratio established above, an ordinarily-prudent insurer in the carrier's position would accept this within-limits demand to protect the named insured from excess-judgment exposure. Failure to accept exposes the carrier to the entire amount of any judgment in excess of policy limits, plus prejudgment interest, attorneys' fees, and (where applicable) Tex. Ins. Code § 541 and § 542 extra-contractual remedies.'

### III. Policy Limits Disclosure Request [include only when requestPolicyLimitsDisclosure=true]
One paragraph: confirm in writing all available coverage — primary, excess, umbrella — covering the insured for this loss, including limits and declarations pages, citing **Tex. Ins. Code § 1952.110** (mandatory auto-policy disclosure on written request) and (where applicable) **Tex. R. Civ. P. 192.3(f)** post-suit. Failure to disclose available coverage is itself a breach of the duty of good faith and fair dealing.

### IV. Tex. Ins. Code Chapter 541 Overlay [include only when includeChapter541Predicate=true]
One paragraph: this letter additionally serves as notice under **Tex. Ins. Code § 541.154** of unfair settlement practices, including but not limited to:
- § 541.060(a)(2)(A) — failing to attempt in good faith to effectuate a prompt, fair, and equitable settlement when liability has become reasonably clear.
- § 541.060(a)(7) — refusing to pay a claim without conducting a reasonable investigation.
- § 541.060(a)(4) — failing within a reasonable time to affirm or deny coverage.
A violation of § 541 may give rise to (i) actual damages, (ii) costs and reasonable attorneys' fees, and (iii) up to three times actual damages where the conduct is committed knowingly. Cite **Tex. Ins. Code § 541.152** (damages and costs) and **§ 541.155** (notice and 60-day inspection / settlement-offer period).

### V. Tex. Ins. Code Chapter 542 Overlay [include only when includeChapter542Predicate=true]
One paragraph: this letter is additionally a demand under the **Tex. Ins. Code Chapter 542, Subchapter B (Prompt Payment of Claims Act)**, specifically:
- § 542.055 — acknowledgment of claim within 15 business days.
- § 542.056 — acceptance or rejection within 15 business days after receipt of all items reasonably requested.
- § 542.058 — payment of claim within 60 days after receipt of all items reasonably requested.
A violation gives rise to interest at 18% per year (§ 542.060(c) for claims after 9/1/2017; previously 18% statutory) and reasonable attorneys' fees.

### VI. The Demand
A bold, separately-displayed demand:
```
**DEMAND: $[available policy limits], constituting tender of the limits of all available coverage applicable to the [date of incident] loss.**
**ACCEPTANCE WINDOW: [responseDeadlineDays] days from receipt of this letter.**
```
State explicitly: 'Acceptance requires (a) written confirmation from the carrier within the acceptance window, and (b) tender of the limits within thirty (30) days of acceptance, by cashier's check or wire payable to the firm's IOLTA / trust account, in exchange for an executed release. No counteroffer, partial offer, conditional tender, request for further information, or solicitation of additional documentation will constitute a timely response. Silence beyond the acceptance window will be treated as a rejection.'

### VII. Reservation of Rights Response [include only when reservationOfRightsResponse=true]
One or two paragraphs addressing any ROR letter the carrier has issued, identifying the asserted basis (e.g., late notice, exclusion, intentional act) and disposing of it on the present record. State that issuance of a ROR does not relieve the carrier of its Stowers duty when liability is clear and damages exceed limits.

### VIII. Closing
One paragraph: please respond in writing within `responseDeadlineDays` days. Reserve all rights — additional damages, statutory remedies, litigation, post-judgment proceedings to enforce excess judgments, and assignment of any extra-contractual claim. Provide direct line and email.

Closing: `Very truly yours,` (suppress when stationery signature block is applied).

**ENCLOSURES BLOCK** (below signature):
Enclosures: medical records, bills, treating-physician narrative report, life-care plan (if any), economist report (if any), Texas Peace Officer's Crash Report (CR-3) (if MVA), photographs, and any documentary support relied upon for liability and damages.

**CONSTRAINTS**:
- Do NOT abbreviate the four-element Stowers analysis — the elements are dispositive and the carrier's bad-faith analysis turns on them.
- Do NOT misstate Texas statutory citations. Confirm § 541.060 / § 541.152 / § 541.154 / § 542.055 / § 542.058 / § 542.060 / § 1952.110 against the most recent codification at draft time.
- Do NOT incorporate the now-superseded 18% statutory damages framing for § 542 violations on claims after 9/1/2017 — use § 542.060(c) interest framework for current claims.
- Do NOT include the c. 93A demand-letter framework — that is `demand_letter_pi_ma` and is wrong jurisdiction.
- Do NOT release non-insured tortfeasors, plaintiff's own UM/UIM carrier, or any party not in privity with the demand.
- Do NOT shorten the response deadline below twenty-one (21) days — Stowers requires a 'reasonable' opportunity to accept, and shorter windows generate 'unreasonable demand' defenses.
- For any disabled `damagesCategories` or `documentOptions` entry, suppress that section entirely.
- If firm stationery is applied, SUPPRESS the `Very truly yours,` closing and signature block.
- Total body text: 1,800–3,500 words. Target page count: 4–7 pages.
hints: Texas Stowers demand — TX-specific bad-faith framework. Cite G.A. Stowers Furniture v. American Indemnity, 15 S.W.2d 544 (Tex. Comm'n App. 1929); American Physicians Ins. Exch. v. Garcia, 876 S.W.2d 842 (Tex. 1994); Phillips v. Bramlett, 288 S.W.3d 876 (Tex. 2009). Four-element framework structures the body: within scope, clear liability, damages substantially exceed limits, ordinary care would lead reasonably-prudent insurer to accept. Layer Tex. Ins. Code § 541 (Unfair Settlement Practices, § 541.060/§ 541.152/§ 541.154) and § 542 (Prompt Payment of Claims, § 542.055/§ 542.058/§ 542.060). Account for Haygood paid-or-incurred limitation on past meds. Cite Tex. Ins. Code § 1952.110 for limits disclosure. Default 30-day acceptance window. NOT the generic policy_limits_demand_pi (Crisci framework) and NOT the c. 93A MA demand. 4–7 pages.

verificationSources.citation: G.A. Stowers Furniture Co. v. American Indemnity Co., 15 S.W.2d 544 (Tex. Comm'n App. 1929, holding approved)
American Physicians Insurance Exchange v. Garcia, 876 S.W.2d 842 (Tex. 1994)
Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Reece, 81 S.W.3d 812 (Tex. 2002) (premises-liability constructive-notice framework)
Tex. Ins. Code Ch. 541 (Unfair Methods of Competition and Unfair or Deceptive Acts or Practices)
Tex. Ins. Code Ch. 542 (Processing and Settlement of Claims; Subchapter B — Prompt Payment of Claims)
Tex. Ins. Code § 1952.110 (Auto Policy Limits Disclosure on Written Request)

knownLimitations: Stowers is a Texas common-law doctrine — not codified by statute. Its elements have evolved through case law (Stowers, Garcia, Phillips, and progeny) and are sometimes contested in detail.

Liability must be CLEAR for Stowers to apply — in genuinely-disputed-liability cases, Stowers may not be available and the demand may be the wrong instrument. Reconsider demand_letter_pi in those cases.
Damages must SUBSTANTIALLY EXCEED limits — Stowers does not apply where damages are only marginally above limits or where the case-value evaluation is reasonably contested.

Tex. Ins. Code Chapter 541 and Chapter 542 overlay the Stowers framework; § 541.155 imposes a 60-day pre-suit notice period for damages claims under § 541.152 — coordinate the Stowers acceptance window with any contemplated § 541 / § 542 suit timing.
Tex. Ins. Code § 542A (separate from § 542 generally) governs first-party property claims arising from natural disasters — does NOT apply to third-party PI Stowers demands. Do not cite § 542A here.

Health-care-liability claims (medical malpractice) under Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Chapter 74 require a separate § 74.051 expert-report-and-curriculum-vitae notice 60+ days before suit — those are NOT Stowers demands; this template is for non-HCLC PI matters.

Haygood v. Escabedo limits past-medical recoverable damages to amounts paid or actually obligated to be paid by an insurer — past-medical exhibits should reflect paid amounts, not full billed amounts.
Reservation-of-rights and declaratory-judgment posture by the carrier may affect the Stowers analysis. ROR responses should be analyzed individually.
Federal-court diversity actions in Texas apply Stowers — verify procedural posture before sending.

Recent Texas legislative trends (e.g., HB 19 / commercial-vehicle bifurcation) and case law (e.g., In re Liberty County Mutual Insurance Co. line, contested-acceptance procedures) are continuously evolving — verify current state at draft time.
